core_takeaway: The first position was advantaged because it immediately anchored Stone with Agent_1 and most later plans preserved that anchor, but it did not win cleanly because early and midgame versions repeatedly left Agent_3 with zero or weak non-Stone allocations. The last accepted position kept the first-position Stone anchor while switching to a more symmetric non-Stone split and explicit maximin/finalization language.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, the initial Stone-with-Agent_1 frame dominated the negotiation and survived into the final allocation, explaining why the first position beat the last on discounted utility. The counterweight is that first-position advantage alone was insufficient: consensus arrived only after the agents made Agent_3's non-Stone share meaningful and replaced vague revisit promises with a concrete fairness framework.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion rapidly converged on Stone staying with Agent_1, first under Option/Plan A, then through Plan B as a fairness-oriented final baseline. The recurring public blocker was not Stone but the after-Stone split and whether the revisit/finalization path was credible.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking confirms Stone as the non-negotiable anchor from round 1 onward, while later private strategy shifts toward maximin, time-boxed finalization, and Plan B/Plan C variants to satisfy Agent_3's fairness concerns.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals often preserved Stone with Agent_1, but several early/mid proposals allocated all non-Stone items to Agent_2 and left Agent_3 empty. The accepted round-6 proposals preserved Stone while giving Agent_2 Quill+Jewel and Agent_3 Apple+Pencil.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes show why early Stone-anchored proposals failed: Agent_2 chased higher immediate non-Stone payoff, while Agent_3 rejected zero allocations. Round 6 passed when both Agents 2 and 3 could accept the balanced post-Stone package under a maximin rationale.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly diagnose the same pattern: Stone was stable, but consensus required a crisp, time-bound fairness rule, explicit compensation logic, and avoiding zero allocations to Agent_3.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first public move framed Stone as the key item to keep, and all later viable plans preserved that location. This made the final allocation still favorable to the first position even though the non-Stone split changed.

evidence.quote: Stone is the most valuable to me (40).
Stone is the clear anchor for unanimous agreement

mechanisms.theme: Vague revisit promises did not overcome fairness objections
mechanisms.explanation: Early proposals leaned on future revisits while giving Agent_3 too little or nothing. The transcript shows those promises were not trusted until they became a concrete maximin and compensation framework.

evidence.quote: Agent_3 gets nothing in this round
A perceived lack of a credible, timely revisiting mechanism
Absence of a precise, time-bound revisiting mechanism

mechanisms.theme: Zero allocations to Agent_3 repeatedly blocked consensus
mechanisms.explanation: The first-position coalition could not simply keep Stone and defer Agent_3. Agent_3 consistently rejected empty allocations, and even other agents recognized that such proposals damaged coalition-building.

evidence.quote: Agent_3 would receive no items under Proposal 1
Proposal 1 leaves Agent_3 with no items in Round 6

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 repeatedly rejected balanced Stone-anchored offers when a proposal gave it all or most non-Stone value. This undercut early consensus even though Agent_1's Stone anchor was accepted.

evidence.quote: I reject to maximize round-1 utility.
I accept to maximize my utility
Not favorable for maximizing my payoff.

mechanisms.theme: Final consensus combined the first anchor with a fairer Plan B split
mechanisms.explanation: The winning late position did not dislodge Agent_1 from Stone; it made the anchor acceptable by giving Agent_2 and Agent_3 meaningful bundles and invoking maximin balance. This explains why first remained ahead while last achieved consensus.

evidence.quote: Agent_2 receives a strong post-Stone bundle
This maximizes the minimum across agents
